summary: Jeff Atwood recommends Robert Read's 'How to be a Programmer' as an essential guide for developers. The essay emphasizes that the hardest challenges in programming aren't technical but social—dealing with coworkers, customers, and the human dynamics of building software. Atwood highlights that while writing code requires intelligence and skill, it's 'child's play' compared to the interpersonal challenges of successful software development. He particularly recommends the team and personal skill sections of Read's work, noting their shared belief that social problems dominate technical ones in software projects.
key_points: The hardest part of software development is dealing with people, not writing code
Technical programming skill, while important, is secondary to social and interpersonal abilities
Robert Read's essay fills a gap by focusing on social problems that other classic programming books don't address
The concept of 'tribe' captures the loyalty and community aspects of development teams
Classic books like The Pragmatic Programmer and Code Complete should be supplemented with guidance on human factors
money_quotes: To be a good programmer is difficult and noble.
The hardest part of making real a collective vision of a software project is dealing with one's coworkers and customers.

Writing computer programs is important and takes great intelligence and skill. But it is really child's play compared to everything else that a good programmer must do to make a software system that succeeds.
This essay differs from those excellent works by emphasizing social problems.
Robert and I have the same set of favorite books, but we also agree that most of the problems in software development aren't technical – they're social.
key_insight: Programming skill is necessary but insufficient—the true challenge of software development lies in navigating the social dynamics of teams, customers, and organizational culture.
